title: Chapter One: The Prophecy
content:
> *"The Baihui acupoint sits at the crown of the skull -- the gate between heaven and the self. In most practitioners, this gate opens a crack. In the rarest cases, it opens wide. And in those who are truly cursed, it never closes at all."*
> --Erta Brightleaf, *Treatise on the Nine Yin Constitutions*, Volume III

---

The night I was born, a stranger knocked on my father's gate.

I don't remember this, obviously -- I was busy being a newborn, which I'm told involves a lot of screaming and very little strategic thinking. But the servants talked, when they thought I couldn't hear. They always talked.

Moonlight on the river. An otherworldly face. Ears like long leaves, tapering to elegant points -- an Elf, walking the backroads of Henan Province like he'd gotten spectacularly lost on his way to somewhere that mattered. He smelled like crushed grass, the servants said, like the wild had followed him in through the door.

He offered my father a trade: one night's lodging for a medical consultation.

My father -- Jeong Dae-myeong, head of the Jeong family, lord of approximately one county's worth of dirt and soybeans -- happened to have a newborn son he was already regretting. My mother, his third wife, had died bringing me into the world. A difficult labor, they called it. As if *difficult* could contain the whole ugly truth of a woman bleeding out while her husband stood in the next room deciding whether the child was worth the cost.

He wasn't sure it was.

So he brought the Elf to my cradle. And the stranger looked down at me -- really looked, with those ageless eyes that had probably watched dynasties rise and rot -- and delivered his diagnosis with the bedside manner of a man who'd run out of patience centuries ago.

"It'll be hard for this one to live past twenty. That's the constitution."

Twenty.

*Twenty.*

My father, because he was exactly the kind of man you'd expect to rule a backwater estate through sheer pigheaded stubbornness, heard this and immediately made it about himself.

"An early death? This child was born after I disgraced its mother. Is this divine punishment?"

The Elf's face twisted -- not with pity, but with the specific disgust of someone watching a man step on a rake for the third time.

"Is that what you ask a doctor? Even for an ignorant country bumpkin--"

"What about the child's condition?" My father cleared his throat. Even in my origin story, he couldn't manage to stay on topic.

The Elf sighed. "As a father, I'll tell you. This is similar to the Nine Yin Severed Pulse. I've never seen someone with such an extremely open Baihui acupoint. It's like the top of the head is completely open."

"I thought the Nine Yin Severed Pulse was about dying from hardened meridians."

"You're not entirely ignorant." The Elf stroked one long ear -- a habit, maybe, or maybe just something to do with his hands while he resisted the urge to slap my father. "This child will pass away due to excessively open meridians at the crown. It's commonly called ascension."

"Ascension? Isn't that when a Taoist master with high spiritual power departs for the celestial realm?"

"You believe in such things?" The Elf's voice went flat. "I wonder if you've heard of some absolute masters using the upper Dantian of the head. Ascension is when one is intoxicated by the energy of nature pouring down from heaven and earth, ultimately unable to bear it and crossing the Sanzu River. It's a distorted story."

The Sanzu River. The river between the living and the dead. My ticket was apparently already booked.

"It seems it is divine punishment after all," my father said, because *of course* he circled back to that.

"You turtle of a man. To think you're a father--"

My father threw him out after that. Drew himself up to his full height, pointed a finger, demanded to know the stranger's rank in the murim, the martial world -- as if social hierarchy could cure what was wrong with me, as if knowing whether the Elf outranked his great-grandfather's great-grandfather would change the number *twenty* into something larger.

The Elf left. The conversation didn't spread beyond the estate walls.

But here's the part that sits in my chest like a stone, the part the servants whispered when they thought I was asleep and too young to understand:

Standing outside the gate, looking back at the estate one last time, the Elf shook his head.

"If raised in an unorthodox sect, this child would have become a demon. If grown in Shaolin, the 72 arts would have doubled. But born as a hated child in such a backwater place, there's no other way but to die young in this countryside."

A pause. The wind off the river.

"Unless they awaken on their own."

---

Fifteen years later, I still haven't decided if that was a prophecy or a punchline.

Here's what I know: My name is Jeong Yeon-shin. I am the third son of the Jeong family of Xinye County, Henan Province. My mother is dead because I was born. My father has spent my entire life looking through me like I'm a window he'd rather keep shuttered. My brothers think I'm a joke. The servants think I'm cursed.

And somewhere inside my skull, the gate between heaven and earth is wide open, and the flood is coming, and there is nothing -- *nothing* -- I've found so far that can close it.

I'm fifteen years old. I'm standing in the training ground behind the east wing, where the packed earth smells like dust and iron filings and the faint sweetness of the soybean fields beyond the estate walls. The evening air is warm and heavy -- the kind of Henan summer heat that sits on your shoulders like a wet cloth and refuses to leave.

I have roughly five years left, though I don't know that yet. Not for certain. That particular gut-punch is still a few hours away.

For now, all I have is this training ground, the wooden pillar across my shoulders, and the strange, relentless fire in my brain that makes me take things apart and put them back together better. I adjust my grip on the pillar, feel the rough grain bite into my palms, and lower myself into another squat.

The clock is ticking. It has always been ticking.

I just haven't learned to hear it yet.
